title: Snow and Ice Roof Damage Insurance Claims in Lansing, MI | Commercial Roofing Contractors of Lansing
metaDescription: Documenting ice dam and snow-load roof damage on Lansing commercial buildings before the thaw erases the evidence needed for an insurance claim.

html:
Documenting Ice Dam and Snow-Load Roof Damage Before the Thaw Erases It

Mid-Michigan winters put two distinct kinds of stress on a commercial roof: ice damming at parapet walls and roof edges, and the sheer weight of wet, heavy snow accumulating faster than it sublimates. Neither one announces itself right away. Ice damming backs water up under the membrane at the first thaw, sometimes weeks after the storm that caused it. Snow-load stress shows up as strained seams and flashing that fail quietly before a leak ever reaches the interior. Both patterns make winter claims some of the hardest to document well, because the visible evidence changes as fast as the weather does.

How Ice Damming Actually Damages a Roof

Ice builds up at parapet walls, roof edges, and around rooftop equipment where snow melts unevenly and refreezes. That buildup creates a dam that backs meltwater up under the membrane at the first warm spell, and by the time water shows up inside the building, the ice that caused it may be long gone. Older gravel and modified bitumen roofs on downtown state buildings and campus-era construction are especially exposed to this pattern, since their edge details were often built to a lower standard than current membrane systems require.

Snow-Load Stress on Larger Roof Spans

Wet, heavy snow does structural work through weight rather than ice, and it hits hardest on the wide, low-slope roofs common over GM's assembly and distribution buildings along the Delta Township and Grand River corridors. A roof carrying an unusually heavy snow load can develop seam and flashing stress that is not visible from the ground and does not always show up as a leak until a later thaw. We inspect roofs that carried a heavy snow event as soon as safe access allows, rather than waiting for water to announce a problem that has already been building for weeks.

Why Documentation Timing Matters More in Winter

An ice dam or snow-load claim is only as strong as the record made close to the event. Once a thaw happens, the ice is gone, the water has already moved through whatever path it found, and reconstructing where the damage originated becomes a matter of inference rather than direct evidence. We photograph roof edges and drainage while ice or heavy snow is still present when access allows, and we document interior water intrusion against the roof condition record so the connection between the two is established rather than assumed.

Building the Scope for a Winter Claim

A winter damage scope typically separates two things: the interior repair from water that already got through, and the roof-level fix that stops it from recurring on the next thaw. Edge metal and flashing details that failed once under an ice dam are likely to fail again under the same conditions unless the underlying detail is corrected, and not simply patched over. We itemize both pieces of the scope so the claim reflects the full picture rather than just the visible interior damage.

Not Every Winter Problem Is a Claim

Some roof issues that show up in late winter are gradual deterioration rather than storm-related damage, and we say so when that is what the roof shows. Coverage typically depends on whether the cause was a specific event or ordinary wear, and an honest assessment protects the credibility of every claim we help document, this one included.

Documenting the Difference, Not Deciding the Claim

We're your roofing contractor, not a public adjuster — we document and substantiate the roof damage so you and your adjuster work from an accurate scope. We do not promise a claim outcome, and we separate storm-caused damage from pre-existing wear as clearly as the roof allows.

Snow and Ice Damage Questions From Lansing Owners

How soon after an ice storm or heavy snow should the roof be inspected?

As soon as it is safe to access. Ice dam damage in particular can sit hidden until a thaw makes it visible as an interior leak, so an inspection while conditions are still fresh captures evidence that disappears once the ice melts.

Does insurance typically cover ice dam damage?

Often, if the damage is tied to a specific weather event rather than years of gradual wear at the same edge detail. That distinction is exactly what a documentation record close to the event needs to establish.

Can snow-load damage happen without any visible roof failure?

Yes. Seam and flashing stress from a heavy snow load can develop without an immediate leak, which is why we recommend inspecting roofs that carried an unusually heavy event even if nothing has shown up inside yet.

Will you document damage from a claim months after the storm?

We can, but the record is weaker the further out it gets from the event, since ice and snow evidence is gone and interior damage may have additional causes layered on top. Documenting close to the event produces the strongest record.

What if the roof damage turns out to be pre-existing wear, not storm-related?

We tell owners that directly rather than characterizing ordinary deterioration as event damage. An accurate assessment, even when it is not the answer an owner was hoping for, holds up better than an inflated one.
